assignment.name: calibration
assignment.title: Model AI's effect on one job category
assignment.description: Build a small agent-based simulation (15-20 workers) that models how AI adoption disrupts and reshapes one job category. The most critical early decision is your implementation tool: NetLogo (the standard ABM platform for this course), a general-purpose language (Python with Mesa), or a spreadsheet (Excel or Google Sheets). After choosing the tool, select a job category and define the emergent mechanism. Compare exactly two scenarios: high automation pressure vs. high worker support. Deliver a 1-page memo and one figure (diagram or screenshot).
constraints: Must compare exactly two scenarios: high automation pressure vs. high worker support.
15-20 worker agents, at least 3 discrete states, 1 explicit emergent mechanism.
Must distinguish programmed behavior from emergent outcomes in the interpretation.
AI use must be documented with exact prompts and what was changed manually.

recommended_plan: NetLogo (built-in ABM, visual interface, easy state tracking)
Data entry clerks: emergent mechanism = productivity cliff (workers compete on speed/accuracy against AI, triggering layoffs when threshold crossed)

verdict.notes: Excellent tool choice and well-anchored domain with a genuinely emergent mechanism (productivity cliff as a threshold crossing, not a programmed rule). The only minor risk is whether the student can articulate the distinction between the threshold logic (programmed) and the cascading layoff dynamics (emergent) clearly enough in a 1-page memo—but the plan itself is rock-solid for 7 days.

child.chosen_best_option: Data entry clerks: emergent mechanism = productivity cliff (workers compete on speed/accuracy against AI, triggering layoffs when threshold crossed)

branches.option: Customer service representatives: emergent mechanism = skill obsolescence (workers lose relevance as AI handles routine queries, forcing retraining or exit)

history: NetLogo (built-in ABM, visual interface, easy state tracking)
Customer service representatives: emergent mechanism = skill obsolescence (workers lose relevance as AI handles routine queries, forcing retraining or exit)

verdict.notes: NetLogo is the right tool and skill obsolescence is genuinely emergent, but customer service is too narrow and predictable a domain—the automation story is obvious and the feedback loops lack the richness needed to challenge a 7-day assignment. Consider pivoting to a role with more complex task heterogeneity (e.g., software developers, financial analysts, or radiologists) where AI disrupts some tasks but creates new work, generating real strategic tension and worker stratification.

branches.option: Radiologists/medical imaging analysts: emergent mechanism = workflow bifurcation (AI handles routine cases, humans handle edge cases, creating bottleneck or skill stratification)

history: NetLogo (built-in ABM, visual interface, easy state tracking)
Radiologists/medical imaging analysts: emergent mechanism = workflow bifurcation (AI handles routine cases, humans handle edge cases, creating bottleneck or skill stratification)

verdict.notes: Excellent tool choice and conceptually sound domain with genuine emergent potential. The workflow bifurcation mechanism is implementable in NetLogo and will naturally produce stratification without hard-coding it—radiologists observing AI case filtering will organically shift to edge-case handling. Only minor risk: radiologists as a domain is narrower than multi-role technical teams, and the memo must carefully distinguish which outcomes (skill sorting vs. bottleneck severity) are emergent vs. initialized; if the student conflates setup with emergence, quality drops to 0.72.

The only minor risk is whether the student can articulate the distinction between the threshold logic (programmed) and the cascading layoff dynamics (emergent) clearly enough in a 1-page memo—but the plan itself is rock-solid for 7 days.

branches.option: Python with Mesa (more flexible, better for custom mechanics, steeper learning curve)

history: Python with Mesa (more flexible, better for custom mechanics, steeper learning curve)

verdict.notes: Python/Mesa is a defensible choice—more flexible than NetLogo for custom mechanics—but the learning curve eats into 7 days unless you've already coded in it. The emergent mechanism (skill obsolescence → retraining bottleneck → service quality collapse) is conceptually sound and genuinely emergent, not hard-coded. Customer service is a solid domain, but the plan risks being too linear (skill drops → retrain → skill recovers) unless you add role stratification or competitive dynamics to deepen the model. Expect feasibility to hinge on Mesa setup speed; scope fit depends on whether you can avoid getting bogged down in framework details and actually build the mechanism by day 4.

branches.option: Radiology/medical imaging (emergent: radiologist workflow fragmentation → diagnostic uncertainty → hybrid human-AI decision paralysis)

history: Python with Mesa (more flexible, better for custom mechanics, steeper learning curve)
Radiology/medical imaging (emergent: radiologist workflow fragmentation → diagnostic uncertainty → hybrid human-AI decision paralysis)

verdict.notes: Python/Mesa is a defensible choice and radiology offers genuine emergent complexity, but the learning curve creates real timeline risk for a 7-day sprint. The emergent mechanism—decision paralysis from workflow fragmentation—is conceptually strong but risks becoming a hard-coded state transition rather than truly emergent from agent interactions. NetLogo would trade some flexibility for certainty of delivery.

branches.option: Truck driving (emergent: geographic job displacement → urban/rural migration pressure → labor market bifurcation)

history: Python with Mesa (more flexible, better for custom mechanics, steeper learning curve)
Truck driving (emergent: geographic job displacement → urban/rural migration pressure → labor market bifurcation)

verdict.notes: Python/Mesa is a defensible choice and truck driving offers genuine emergent complexity (geographic stratification + labor market bifurcation), but the learning curve on Mesa will compress your modeling window. You'll spend 1-2 days on boilerplate and debugging instead of on scenario comparison rigor. NetLogo would give you 4-5 full days of uninterrupted modeling; Mesa gives you 2-3. The emergent mechanism is sound, but execution risk is higher.

branches.option: Spreadsheet with manual agent tracking (fastest to prototype, limited scalability)

history: Spreadsheet with manual agent tracking (fastest to prototype, limited scalability)

verdict.notes: Spreadsheet implementation for agent-based simulation is the wrong tool—setup friction and debugging will consume most of your 7 days, leaving little time for actual scenario comparison and interpretation. The retail cashier domain is conceptually sound and bifurcation is genuinely emergent, but the tool choice makes this plan fail on both execution and scope fit.

Customer service representatives: AI chatbot deployment → emergent outcome is skill-based sorting (high-skill workers migrate to complex cases, low-skill workers face unemployment risk)

verdict.notes: Spreadsheet is the wrong tool for agent-based simulation—setup friction and debugging will consume most of your 7 days, leaving little time for scenario comparison or interpretation. Customer service is also a narrow, low-complexity domain where the automation story is deterministic, not emergent: you'll end up hard-coding skill sorting rather than observing it emerge from agent interactions.

branches.option: Data entry clerks: AI document processing → emergent outcome is organizational restructuring (remaining workers become AI validators, creating bottleneck effects on throughput)

history: Spreadsheet with manual agent tracking (fastest to prototype, limited scalability)
Data entry clerks: AI document processing → emergent outcome is organizational restructuring (remaining workers become AI validators, creating bottleneck effects on throughput)

verdict.notes: Spreadsheet is the wrong tool for agent-based simulation—setup friction and debugging will consume most of the 7 days, leaving insufficient time for scenario comparison and interpretation. Even with only 15–20 agents, manual tracking in Excel/Sheets makes it nearly impossible to implement true emergence; you'll spend days on bookkeeping instead of modeling. The conceptual framing (validator bottleneck) is sound, but the tool choice will prevent you from realizing it.
